creative_brief: Hero image: a parent and child at a kitchen counter unpacking a brown paper bag of vegetables, with a small farm-name sticker visible on a bunch of carrots. Warm natural light, real kitchen — not styled. Below-fold imagery: close-up of a hub collection point with labelled bags ready for pickup. Earthy palette, no stock photography.
headline: I kept telling my kids our food was fresh. It wasn't.
subhead: The lettuce had travelled further than our last family holiday. The beef had no farm name on the label. I stopped pretending — and helped build a grocery store that doesn't need you to. The investor waitlist closes before we launch.
hero_copy: I stood in the produce aisle and watched my daughter put spinach in the bag. She asked if it was from a farm. I said yes — because technically it was. But I had no idea which farm. No idea how many warehouses it passed through. No idea how many weeks it sat in cold storage. I just knew the label said 'fresh' and I wanted to believe it. FarmThru exists because families like mine deserve a grocery store where 'fresh' means straight from farm to hub — not a marketing word on plastic wrap. And right now, you can own a piece of it before we open.

sections.heading: The system I kept funding every Tuesday night
sections.body: Industry data suggests supermarket produce can travel around 2,400 kilometres before it reaches the shelf. That is ten times the distance of a farm-direct supply chain. Your beef has no farm name on the label. Your tomatoes were picked hard and gassed to look ripe. Farmers — the people actually growing your food — get squeezed at every step by middlemen who add cost but not quality. I knew all of this. I still swiped my card every week. Because there was no real alternative. No store that cut the unnecessary middlemen, partnered directly with farms, and let me collect groceries I could actually trace. So we built one.

sections.heading: What FarmThru actually changes
sections.body: FarmThru is a farm-direct grocery store. We partner with roughly 15 to 20 farms and suppliers across NSW. You order online. You collect from our Brookvale hub, Monday to Friday. No membership fees. No subscription. No lock-in — order when you want. The supply chain runs from farm to hub to you. That is it. No warehouses stacking weeks onto your meat. No anonymous supply chains hiding where your vegetables grew. Same cut of meat, completely different impact on the farmers who raised it. Bulk buying keeps prices honest — you save by buying in the quantities that suit your family, and farmers get paid what the food is actually worth. Your values and your money finally pointing the same direction.

sections.heading: The window to own a piece is narrowing
sections.body: FarmThru is raising capital through equity crowdfunding on Birchal. Minimum investment is $50. Maximum is $10,000 per investor. This is not a donation — you become a part-owner of a grocery store that pays farmers first. You can be a customer and an investor. Both. But the campaign has a cap, and once it fills, it closes. Right now, a $5 refundable deposit gets you VIP access: first look at the offer, priority updates, and an exclusive Q&A before the campaign goes public. No commitment beyond that. You are not locked in. But the people who move first get access first. That is how scarcity works — not as a trick, but as a fact. There are only so many seats at this table.

sections.heading: What you are actually deciding
sections.body: You can keep walking into the same supermarket, buying the same anonymous produce, and hoping it is better than it looks. Or you can be part of building something that does not exist yet — a grocery store where every dollar traces back to a real farm with a real name. One where your family collects food that travelled a fraction of the distance. One where you vote for change with your wallet. The VIP waitlist is open now. It will not be open forever.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru's equity crowdfunding campaign is hosted on Birchal. Investing in startups and early-stage businesses involves risks, including illiquidity, lack of dividends, loss of investment, and dilution. It should only be considered as part of a diversified portfolio. This page is not financial advice. The $5 VIP deposit is fully refundable and does not constitute an investment or obligation to invest.
